prompt: Aerial bird's-eye view looking straight down at nine small square patches of soil arranged in a slightly asymmetric 3x3 grid — uneven organic spacing, like a real garden, not a perfect diagram. Six patches have pale grey-white cracked earth — dry, depleted, dead. The cracks run deep. The soil has never been nourished. Each of these six damaged patches contains one specific object half-buried in the cracked earth — visible, specific, heartbreaking: First patch: a half-torn family photograph, the tear running directly through the middle of the people in it, one half missing entirely, the remaining half face-up in the dust, edges yellowed and curling. Second patch: a single child's shoe worn completely through at the sole — the kind worn long past the point any parent should have replaced it, laces frayed and knotted back together. Third patch: a small child's shirt, faded, repeatedly mended — patches sewn over old patches, folded flat into cracked ground. Fourth patch: a cracked smartphone screen — shattered from corner to corner, face down in the grey dust, the only window to the outside world, broken. Fifth patch: a pair of white earphones knotted tightly, tangled around themselves — the only private world, the only escape, half-buried in the cracked earth. Sixth patch: a SIM card snapped cleanly in two — both halves visible in the dust, the last connection severed. The saplings growing in these six damaged patches are stunted, pale, twisted: one bent completely sideways, one barely a thread breaking the surface, one a dry brittle twig with no leaves, one with yellowed leaves curled inward, two still alive but growing at wrong angles, reaching for something that is not there. Cold grey-blue forensic light falls on all six damaged patches. Clinical. Unforgiving. Three patches have dark rich brown soil — moist, warm, alive. Nothing buried here. Just clean earth and roots that found water. The saplings in these three are small but upright. Straight. Green. Slightly desaturated — survivors, not paradise. Warm amber light falls only on these three. One single outlier: one damaged patch whose sapling is bent sideways but still faintly green — leaning toward the nearest healthy patch. Not there yet. Not stopped. The ratio is countable without trying: six damaged, three alive. Two out of every three. The fact is in the ground. Style: hyper-realistic forensic macro photography. Medium format film. Shot from directly above, completely flat, clinical and cold. Every crack in the dry soil razor sharp. Every artifact clearly visible and specific — the shattered screen, the snapped SIM card, the knotted earphones. Extreme detail throughout. The boundary between cold and warm light cuts organically — not a straight line. Not ecological. Not environmental. This is about what was buried in the soil that children were given to grow in. No people. No faces. No text. No labels. No symbols. No borders. This is documentation of something that should not be this common. Photorealistic. Not illustrated. Not painterly. Real macro photography aesthetic.
dataset_name: CDC BRFSS Adverse Childhood Experiences Module
insight: Two in three adults have experienced at least one adverse childhood experience — abuse, neglect, or household dysfunction — with effects that compound and persist across their entire lives.
